speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, Ursula, would you continue with the lessons that you would like people to know? And you were just telling us what some of the students wanted to know from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, they-- they asked me how I-- I felt about having had these experiences. Am I ashamed? Is it a punishment from God? Or do I feel proud or special? I don't feel ashamed. I don't feel in any way that it is a punishment from God. Nor do I feel that I was singled out. I feel that I survived physically and mentally and emotionally, first of all because of luck, because of genetics. I was strong and healthy. I was fortunate to have had people in Holland, Mr and Mrs. Van Mackelenbergh, who helped us-- the people, Sister Renaldas, Rector Wijers, the people in Eersel who helped us, our friends there. All the people of Eersel were so kind to us. Because of their help, we were able to survive physically as well as emotionally, and I was able to find some sort of balance. I do, however, feel proud not to have survived or feel specially singled out by a superior being to have survived, no, not at all. What I do feel proud of is to be a member of a group of people who were so tormented, tortured, persecuted, totally humiliated, and killed. And I practically-- my brother and I practically escaped by the skin of our teeth-- that we are members or I am a member of a group of people who suffered so much but who, as a group, do not spend their time seeking revenge. That for the most part, many survivors, I think, are depressed and often I am depressed, but I've learned how to deal with it. But we spend our life in a constructive way, in a way that-- that our children, our grandchildren can learn from our experiences, and that hopefully through this tape and our writings, the future generations will learn from our experiences, that somewhere all of this hatred has to stop. That if we now would be living a life of revenge, that it will go on and on and on, and there would be no end. And I think we, as a group of people, have turned it around. And of course, I'm very happy about the state of Israel. That is another story, but I am very happy about that victory for our people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know you mentioned that you had written a memoir. And-- and that you were doing this video for your children as well. Is there one last statement that you would like to make to them and maybe future grandchildren about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have written in the memoirs what-- what my thoughts are. It's been a complicated story. And I think if they would read it, that it will stimulate some answers in them. That we, you know, had a beautiful family. The one thing George told me was we had a wonderful-- wonderful parents. And probably the survivors, I think, have been able to live constructive, happy lives because of their nurturing in early childhood. I had four years of it, but it is something that stayed with me. And that is so important to give to children, that love, that caring, that nurturing, that I think both George and I received. Unfortunately, after, that we didn't have our parents any longer. But those early beginnings helped us through the rest of our lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the last family picture we took together. On the left is my mother, Lucia Levy. Next to her in back is my father, Max Levy. And to my father's right is my brother, George Levy. And there I am in the middle, Ursula Levy. The picture was taken when I was two years old in 1937 in Lippstadt in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of Mr. Joseph Van Mackelenbergh. It was probably taken during the early 1950s in 's-Hertogenbosch in Holland. He was the man who brought many Jewish children out of Germany including my brother and me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Mrs. Agnes Van Mackelenbergh, the wife of Mr. Joseph Van Mackelenbergh. This picture was probably taken also in the early 1950s in 's-Hertogenbosch, Holland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a group picture of Sister Therese. She is the nun on the left. She was one of the nuns who worked at the children's home with the boys. Next to her is Miss Fief Van Mackelenbergh. She was one of the nurses who worked at the-- the children's home, a relative of Mr. and Mrs. Van Mackelenbergh. Then right in front of her, the tall young man is Klaus Reichenbach. To his left is Hans Reichenbach. On the lower right is Rolf Reichenbach, and then next to him, the young man with the checkered shirt is my brother, George Levy. And then to his right I am, Ursula Levy. This was taken in 1940 in front of [DUTCH], which was part of the-- which was the children's home connected to the convent. And the picture was taken in Eersel, Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a-- a picture of me when I was four years old. It was taken in Eersel in back of the playroom, which was part of the children's home in Eersel, Holland. I was four years old at that time in 1939. And as you can see, the nuns had high hopes for me to be a nun. I had just been baptized. And they made a little nun's outfit for me. This picture was put on the cover of a book that was written by Alma Verhey, V-E-R-H-E-Y. And the name of her book is [SPEAKING DUTCH]. It deals with the hidden children in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a-- a picture of the convent at Saint Jacobusklooster in Eersel, Holland where the children's home is attached to the left there. The building on the left was the children's home. The little building on the right was the rectory. And in the center the nuns lived in the convent. They had a few other people living there also. Many Jewish children, about 50 of us, were hidden there. There were also some prisoners of war. The Mother Renaldas, who was the Mother Superior there, bravely hid many people there, so did Rector Leo Wijers. The building is not standing anymore. It had to be torn down a few years ago. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of Ilse Brul. She was one of the Jewish girls at the convent. Both of her parents were Jewish, and she came from Innsbruck, Austria. She was taken to the concentration camp in 1943. And she died in Auschwitz in September of 1943, '42, excuse me. I'm sorry. I made a mistake. She was taken to the camp in 1942 and died in Auschwitz, September of 1942. This picture is in the same book, the one that I wrote and that was translated into Dutch, [NON-ENGLISH] by Ursula Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the remarkable thing about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was remarkable about her was that she strongly identified with her Jewish heritage, with her parents, and her religion. And she is really a martyr of the Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of Rector Leo Wijers, the rector of the convent, the Saint Jacobusklooster in Eersel, Holland. Next to him stands my brother, George Levy, and I am next to my brother, Ursula Levy. This picture was taken after we came back from Bergen-Belsen. It was the early part of 1946. As you can see, my hair is still very short at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my brother, George Levy, and me, Ursula Levy. It was taken on the day we arrived in the United States, April 14, 1947. And the picture appeared in the on that day, April 14, 1947. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my aunt and uncle, Dr. Joseph Mueller and his wife Irmgard Mueller. It was taken in Chicago, Illinois in 1982. My aunt and uncle raised my brother and me after we came to Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The top document, the top half of the page, is the Entlaffungsschein from the Commandant's office of the concentration camp Sachsenhausen in Germany. What it says there is the entlaffungsschein, which is the dismissal, the dismissal of my father. It is dated December 22, 1938. It says that Max Levy, the Jew, Max Levy, who was born on July 2, 1882 was in Sachsenhausen from November 12, 1938 to December 22, 1938. My father was sent home then on December 22, 1938, after about five weeks at Sachsenhausen. And he died shortly after he came home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my children, Gerard Moeller on the right and Kareen Moeller on the left. It was taken in-- in our home in Skokie, Illinois. And it was Gerard's 16th birthday. It was 1975.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't have an original picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have an original picture with me here. But they are very nice looking children, and I wrote the book for them.
